query: effectiveness of written information sheets on patient recall in total hip arthroplasty

retrieved_docs.title: Total Hip Arthroplasty Techniques -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Nurses need to provide coordination and monitoring of the patient pre-, peri-, and postoperatively with rapid reporting to the clinicians if there are changes in vital signs or evidence of infections. The nurses should also assist the clinicians in providing patient and family education. The pharmacist should evaluate the patient for potential drug-drug interactions, allergic reactions, and assist with appropriate dosing for pain management. Clinicians including the surgeon, nurse practitioner, and physician assistant should work together to provide the patient and family with education regarding the procedure, expected issues, and guidance for aftercare. Only a coordinated effort of the interprofessional team will result in the best outcomes. Level of evidence: II-IV.

retrieved_docs.title: Trochanteric Bursitis (Archived) -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Before initiating treatment, clinicians must educate patients about the possible adverse effects of NSAIDs and corticosteroid injections. Patients should understand the risk-to-benefit ratio when deciding on treatment options. Individuals undergoing THA or joint replacement should receive counseling about the incidence of trochanteric bursitis, which ranges from 5% to 6%.

retrieved_docs.title: Total Hip Arthroplasty Techniques -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Outline the indications for total hip arthroplasty. Describe the technique involved in performing total hip arthroplasty. Review the complications associated with total hip arthroplasty. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance the management of patients with osteoarthritis of the hip. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Periprosthetic Distal Femur Fracture -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Fall prevention is crucial to avoiding fractures in general. The combined efforts of a primary care physician and a surgeon should aim to optimize bone health and limit factors that contribute to falls. Keeping up with the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF) recommendations regarding Dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scanning should be followed. The addition of supplements and/or prescription medication should be implemented as seen fit by the treatment team. Assistive devices should be used by individuals who do not feel stable ambulating alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Femoral Neck Fractures -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Preoperatively, patients should be educated on the limitations on hip movements they may have due to the prosthesis. In addition, emphasis should be placed on proper activities of daily living such as sitting on the toilet, climbing stairs, and sitting and standing from a seated position after surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Prescription of Controlled Substances: Benefits and Risks -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Interprofessional Team Case Study
retrieved_docs.content: Follow-up: Three months after discharge, the patient successfully lost 40 pounds (18 kg) and was cleared for hip replacement surgery. Following the procedure, the patient's pain has dramatically diminished, and she has gradually tapered off her pain medications. She continues to maintain a regular exercise and diet regimen until she notices noticeable improvement. She reports that she feels better, enjoys life, and is encouraged by her progress.

retrieved_docs.title: Hip Joint Injection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Summarize the steps involved in performing a hip joint injection in patients with hip pain and summarize key patient safety points. Describe the typical imaging findings associated with hip joint arthrography. Outline the importance of proper technique when performing a hip joint injection. Review the importance of collaboration between interprofessional team members caring for patients requiring hip joint injections, to improve clinical outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cobalt Toxicity -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Decreasing exposure will ultimately reduce the risk of the development of disease. If you have a metal-on-metal hip arthroplasty, be sure to discuss concerns with your surgeon, particularly if you begin to have new pain, swelling, or difficulty walking, as this may put you at a higher risk of developing toxicity from the implant.

retrieved_docs.title: Hip Precautions -- Issues of Concern -- Hip Precautions
retrieved_docs.content: Most institutions implement a general pre- and post-operative patient educational component which often incorporates these general hip precaution protocols. Education is typically performed by nurses, physical therapists, or physicians. Patients are informed verbally and on some occasions by a video demonstrating hip precautions and body movements to avoid following the surgery. Frequently, patients receive paper handouts listing out hip precautions and diagrams depicting movements to avoid. Many times, these handouts will discuss alternative movements to use. Often time, patients also receive supervised mobility training. The main movements that should be avoided are hip adduction, hip flexion, and external or internal rotation. These precautions are often prescribed for at least 6 weeks or until the soft tissue surrounding the hip has at least partially healed. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Hip Precautions -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians and other healthcare professionals (clinicians, specialists, nurses, physical therapists) who work with hip arthroplasty patients need to understand pre and postoperative hip precautions. These are designed to prevent exacerbation of symptoms and optimize healing and function following various arthroplasty procedures. Interprofessional understanding and communication can improve patient outcomes through coordinated effort and optimal patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: Case Study: 86-Year-Old Male With Leg Swelling After Hip Surgery (Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Acceptable options for repair include open repair via a medial or posterior approach with bypass or interposition and endovascular repair. The decision of which approach for repair will be pursued is dictated by the anatomy of the aneurysm, the surgeon's preference, and patient factors. One meta-analysis of PAA repair reviewing papers comparing outcomes of posterior repair vs. medial repair suggests the superiority of the posterior approach for primary and secondary patency, aneurysm exclusion, and reduced need for reoperation. High-level evidence from randomized clinical trials is needed and would better define the relative benefits of the posterior approach over the medial approach in selected patients. [8] Long-term follow-up is now available for endovascular management of PAA is available and suggests acceptable long-term patency rates. Stent fracture may occur in up to one-third of patients and should be monitored by the vascular specialist. [9] After repair, lifelong follow-up is necessary to monitor the patency of all three approaches. Clinical awareness of this problem and multidisciplinary management can help identify it earlier, allowing for the best patient treatment and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Informed Consent -- Issues of Concern -- Inadequate Documentation
retrieved_docs.content: Mohamed et al conducted a retrospective cross-sectional study examining consent forms for patients undergoing femur fracture operations. Study results revealed inadequate documentation of orthopedic-specific risks. The omissions were believed to be secondary to insufficient orthopedic training for the resident clinicians who most often were tasked with obtaining consent. Corrective measures included training sessions to enhance understanding of standard consent procedures and associated risks. [36] [37] [36]

retrieved_docs.title: Knee Arthroplasty(Archived) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: While knee arthroplasty is done by the orthopedic surgeon, to ensure good outcomes an interprofessional team is required that includes a physical therapist, nurses, an internist, and the primary care provider. After surgery, the patients require physical therapy to regain muscle mass and joint function. In addition, for good outcomes, the patients need to exercise and maintain a healthy weight. Nurses should educate the patient on a healthy diet, avoidance of tobacco and taking part in regular exercise.

retrieved_docs.title: Periprosthetic Proximal Femur Fractures -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Scheduled follow-up appointments in the aging population with their primary care physician should focus on fall prevention and promotion of bone health through exercise and medication. An emphasis on bony surveillance aims to avoid the dreaded complications of a fragility fracture. If a patient suffers a fragility fracture, they should be referred to a primary care or rheumatology physician to manage their bone health and develop a program to decrease the risk of future fractures.

retrieved_docs.title: Synovial Fluid Analysis -- Patient Safety and Education
retrieved_docs.content: While arthrocentesis is well tolerated with minimal complications, obtaining informed consent is a must before performing the procedure. Details of the procedure, risks and benefits, potential complications, and alternatives to therapy/diagnosis should be explained. Patients should understand and be able to verbalize the indications for their own procedure as well as be familiar with the previously discussed complications. Patients must have an opportunity to ask questions, and have those questions answered by the performing physician. Keeping patient safety in mind, previously discussed sterile procedural technique should be used every time to minimize potential risks of infection. The patient's chart should undergo a thorough review prior to undergoing arthrocentesis to ensure the patient has no significant allergies to any medication, material, or chemical that may be in use during the procedure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: Other hip procedures and other knee and hip procedures are organized under the broader category “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified.” Within this hierarchy, the category Other hip procedures is further linked as a subcategory of hip replacement; total and partial, indicating that these procedures involve total or partial hip arthroplasty. Likewise, the category Other knee and hip procedures is associated with arthroplasty knee, reflecting its relationship to knee joint replacement techniques. This classification structure groups orthopedic surgical interventions that do not fit into more specific categories, facilitating accurate coding, documentation, and comprehensive patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1551

In addition, Other hip procedures are also classified as a subset of hip replacement; total and partial, linking them directly to the spectrum of total and partial hip replacement surgeries. This hierarchical organization situates Other hip procedures within both a general framework of miscellaneous interventional procedures and a specific orthopedic context of hip replacement interventions, facilitating clearer clinical classification and patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 523
retrieved_docs.content: Other cardiovascular procedures and other hip procedures are both categorized under the broad group “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified,” indicating that they belong to a general class of medical actions that are not captured by more specific procedure codes. In addition to this shared parent, other cardiovascular procedures are linked to the category “other diagnostic procedures,” reflecting that many of these cardiovascular actions serve diagnostic purposes. Conversely, other hip procedures are connected to “hip replacement; total and partial,” signifying that this subgroup includes various total and partial hip replacement surgeries. Recognizing these hierarchical relationships aids clinicians, coders, and health‑care administrators in accurately classifying, documenting, and managing these procedures within patient care and health‑system reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 561
retrieved_docs.content: Other cardiovascular procedures, other hip procedures, and other knee and hip procedures are all categorized under the broad group “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified.” Within this overarching classification, each of these groups serves as a parent category for more specific types of interventions. Other cardiovascular procedures are linked to other diagnostic procedures, indicating that they encompass cardiovascular interventions that are not otherwise specified and may be associated with diagnostic testing. Other hip procedures are connected to hip replacement—both total and partial—signifying that they include hip surgeries that fall outside standard hip replacement classifications. Other knee and hip procedures are related to arthroplasty knee, showing that they cover knee joint replacement procedures that are not otherwise classified. Together, these relationships illustrate how generic “other” procedure categories are organized within a hierarchical framework of medical interventions, providing a structured way to reference less common or unspecified cardiovascular, hip, and knee procedures in clinical documentation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2646
retrieved_docs.content: Hip head prosthesis (إعاضة رأس الفخذ) and multiple fractures (كسور متعددة) are both recognized as clinical manifestations within the standardized MedDRA query for osteoporosis/low bone mass (هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ)). This relationship highlights that patients with osteoporosis or reduced bone density are prone to severe skeletal complications, including the need for hip head replacement procedures and the occurrence of multiple fractures. Recognizing these associations is important for clinicians to assess fracture risk, consider surgical interventions such as hip prostheses, and implement comprehensive management strategies aimed at preserving bone health and preventing further skeletal injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: Closed fracture of intracapsular section of femur is mapped to fracture of neck of femur (hip), indicating that these two descriptions refer to the same clinical injury of the proximal femur. Fracture of neck of femur (hip) is listed as having a member relationship with هشاشة العظام/قلة العظام (SMQ), the standardized query that groups adverse events related to osteoporosis or low bone density. In the same SMQ, several procedural and injury concepts are also classified as members: تثبيت داخلي للكسر (internal fixation of a fracture), إعاضة رأس الفخذ (femoral head prosthesis or hip replacement), and كسور متعددة (multiple fractures). Together, these relationships show that hip neck fractures, their surgical management (internal fixation or prosthetic replacement), and the occurrence of multiple fractures are all recognized as clinical manifestations or treatments associated with osteoporosis/low bone density, underscoring the importance of bone health assessment and appropriate orthopedic intervention in patients with compromised skeletal integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 224
retrieved_docs.content: biopsy are each identified as types of open‑heart surgery (“جراحة القلب المفتوح”). In the musculoskeletal domain, Other hip procedures are placed under the broader hip replacement category (total and partial), while Other knee and hip procedures are considered a subset of knee arthroplasty (“arthroplasty knee”). Overall, the knowledge base delineates a structured taxonomy in which general operative categories cascade into specific cardiovascular, adjunct vascular, diagnostic, and musculoskeletal interventions. Central nodes such as heart and pericardial biopsies branch into open, transluminal, and excisional techniques, while procedural classifications (e.g., “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified,” “other diagnostic procedures,” “other or heart procedures”) provide a framework for clinical documentation, coding, and comprehensive patient‑care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 1031
retrieved_docs.content: Other cardiovascular procedures and other hip procedures are categorized within the broad group “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified,” which serves as a parent classification for a variety of miscellaneous medical interventions. Within this hierarchy, “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified” also has a child category called “Procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals,” indicating that pharmaceutical‑based interventions are a subset of these uncategorized procedures. The pharmaceutical subset further includes the specific child “administration of inhaled nitric oxide,” a therapeutic technique that delivers nitric oxide gas via inhalation. In addition to their placement under the general “Procedures and interventions, Not Elsewhere Classified” category, other cardiovascular procedures are also linked to the parent group “other diagnostic procedures,” reflecting their role in diagnostic evaluation of the cardiovascular system. Similarly, other hip procedures are associated with the parent category “hip replacement; total and partial,” highlighting their relevance to surgical hip replacement interventions, whether total or partial. This classification framework illustrates how diverse medical procedures—ranging from broad, uncategorized interventions to specific pharmaceutical administrations and specialized diagnostic or surgical categories—are organized to support clear documentation, coding, and patient‑care planning.
